text: BetFrnd's TEMPLATE.md flagged scripts/rename.ts as missing KNOWN_FILES + \bMLabs\b substitution (recommendations #13, #14, #17) but the fix was never implemented in BetFrnd itself. When porting BetFrnd's recommendations back to the MLabs template, always grep BetFrnd's actual code — recommended fixes in TEMPLATE.md aren't a guarantee the fix exists; sometimes lessons stay documented and unimplemented for sprints. Audited 2026-05-23 during template-hardening plan.

When porting learnings from one fork back to a template, the lesson prose often describes a symptom (e.g. 'BetterAuth cookies don't work over HTTP') without naming the load-bearing code line that needs to change. The /mlabs-review pass MUST grep the cited files in the source fork's actual code, not just trust the lesson's narrative. Example from 2026-05-23 template-hardening review: TEMPLATE.md #20 + #28 and learnings 20/64/65/121 all described BetterAuth cookie-domain issues but never pointed at apps/web/src/lib/auth/index.ts:40 where the env.BETTER_AUTH_URL ?? https://$REPLIT_DEV_DOMAIN fallback actually lives. Without reading that file, the original plan missed adding a task for it.

Don't pin Stripe API version in template's stripe-client.ts. The Stripe Node SDK pins its target wire version internally and Stripe maintains backward-compat within a wire version. BetFrnd's shipped stripe-client.ts deliberately omits apiVersion with a comment explaining this; following that precedent. Reversed an in-progress 'pin at /mlabs-code time' decision on 2026-05-23 after reading BetFrnd's actual code. Generalizes: when locking template decisions about 3rd-party SDK config, verify against the SDK's own guidance + the source fork's shipped code, not abstract first-principles reasoning.

Pre-launch marketing pages with mocked dynamic data: prefer a typed config file (src/config/landing.ts) over a feature-flagged hook stub. The hook abstraction is premature when there's no real data source yet; the config-file swap to real data later is a one-file edit per section. Decision made on BetFrnd v3 landing plan 2026-05-13.

When reviewing a UI plan that overhauls existing components, grep for 'No nav link' / 'Intentional' / 'NOTE:' style code comments near the affected files. The MLabs template hides convention rules in comments (e.g. (app)/design/page.tsx says 'No nav link — devs find this route via DESIGN.md. Intentional'). Plans can silently re-violate these unless the reviewer reads the surrounding code.

Deleting an app-router page.tsx leaves a stale .next/types/validator.ts that imports the now-missing page.js, causing tsc to fail until .next is cleared. Add 'rm -rf apps/web/.next' to any task that deletes or moves a Next.js route file before re-running typecheck.

Replit's default Nix env (replit.nix with only pkgs.unzip) lacks the shared libs Playwright's chromium-headless-shell needs (libglib-2.0, libnss3, libX11.*, libgbm, libatk, libdbus, libnspr4 — 14+ in total). Headless Chromium ldd fails before any spec runs. Two adds make /mlabs-qa viable in Replit: (1) extend replit.nix deps with the X11/glib/nss/atk/dbus/gbm packages, (2) bake 'pnpm exec playwright install chromium' into a workflow so the binary is cached. Until both are in, /mlabs-qa in Replit either aborts or falls back to curl-based smoke testing.

When extending a Better-Auth-based template's signup with new required fields (username, age check, etc.), use additionalFields with input:true for signup but input:false post-creation. The default user-update endpoint will otherwise expose every additionalField as patch-able, bypassing rate limits and audit hooks. Editable fields like username need a dedicated server action that runs through the right hooks; immutable fields like is_over_18 stay input:false forever.

Better Auth post-verify hook is emailVerification.afterEmailVerification(user, request) — verified against node_modules/better-auth/dist/api/routes/email-verification.mjs. Fires AFTER the emailVerified flag flips on the user row but BEFORE auto-sign-in. The hook is in the betterAuth() options block, NOT in databaseHooks. Wrap any side-effect call (e.g. signup-bonus credit) in try/catch — a thrown hook BLOCKS verification, which is rarely the desired behavior for downstream gravy.

Mobile needs Server Action parity via /api/v1/ routes (requireUserJSON + ApiError, never redirect). Return currentRevoked: true so mobile can clear tokens and navigate. Admin search predicates: strip leading @ before ilike so admins can type @username naturally.
RSC (app)/layout.tsx: never create (app)/page.tsx — it shadows (marketing)/page.tsx at / for all visitors; authenticated home dashboard needs its own sprint plan instead

Shell max-width pattern: layout <main> provides only padding (px-6 py-6); each page owns its content width via its own max-w-* wrapper. Removing a global max-w from the shell requires patching all existing pages.
Desktop topbar page-title is a net-negative: adds a usePathname() client island for a label that the active sidebar item already communicates. Drop it; topbar = search + bell only.

RSC+client in same file: impossible in Next.js App Router. If a review says 'two exports in one file' with mixed RSC/client, split into separate files (e.g. app-sidebar.tsx RSC + sidebar-nav-items.tsx client). The boundary is the file, not the export.

BetterAuth additionalFields (username, isOver18) are available on session.user from requireUser() — no extra DB query needed for the shell user row. Only fetch fresh from DB when the page is likely to show post-mutation state (e.g. profile page after avatar change).

next-themes inline <script> is load-bearing: removing it (e.g. to silence a React 19 warning) causes FOUC on every route that doesn't hardcode the theme class in its layout. If replacing with custom context, SSR the default theme as a className on <html> in the root layout to avoid the flash.
Playwright globalSetup runs BEFORE webServer boots. Plans that need an authed session in globalSetup can't POST to /api/auth/* — must hit the DB directly or shift work to a per-spec hook.

BetterAuth signs session cookies via Hono's setSignedCookie — cookie value is <session.token>.<HMAC-SHA256>. Writing raw session.token to Playwright storageState won't authenticate. Use auth.api.signInEmail() to mint the cookie, or makeSignature from better-auth/crypto to sign manually.
better-auth/crypto exports public hashPassword, verifyPassword, makeSignature — safe to use for test fixtures and other internal tooling. No need to vendor scrypt or use better-auth internals.

Importing apps/web/src/lib/auth/index.ts in tests is safe even with POSTMARK_SERVER_TOKEN unset — env validator marks it .optional() and the email driver is constructed lazily. As long as nothing in the test flow triggers an actual send, no Postmark side effects.

auth.handler(new Request(...)) is the cleanest way to mint a BetterAuth signed cookie from outside an HTTP context (fixtures, scripts). Avoids depending on undocumented per-endpoint options like {asResponse:true}.

Playwright globalSetup runs in a separate Node process — it does NOT inherit Next.js's automatic .env loading. Use loadEnvConfig from @next/env at the top of the setup file (before any module that reads process.env).

BetterAuth cookie name is HTTPS-conditional: HTTPS baseUrl → __Secure-better-auth.session_token + Secure flag; HTTP → better-auth.session_token. Test fixtures must detect dynamically. On Replit, BETTER_AUTH_URL falls through to REPLIT_DEV_DOMAIN (HTTPS) unless explicitly overridden.

Next.js dev server has a single-instance lock per project dir. Playwright's webServer.command (next dev) cannot start while another next dev is running, even on a different port. For e2e in dev: either stop the local dev server first, or run e2e against the live one (with matching BETTER_AUTH_URL).

Playwright globalSetup runs in plain Node, not Next.js RSC. Files that import  (db/auth/email modules) need NODE_OPTIONS=--conditions=react-server to make Node resolve server-only to its empty.js export. Set this in the npm e2e script.

Wallet number-dominance: bet365-style 90px gold balance + 22px delta-led ledger rows + 36px gold pack numbers carries the home-dashboard visual vocabulary through to the wallet. Winning variant for any sportsbook-adjacent product.

Wallet schema with denormalised balance_vc column: read on every authed page render via getWalletBalance() from the (app) layout. SUM(delta_vc) per-read would be O(N) and N grows forever; denormalised value stays consistent because writes happen inside the same db.transaction as the ledger insert.

MLabs Drizzle id convention: every entity table uses id: text(id).primaryKey().defaultFn(=> crypto.randomUUID()). Service code never passes id in inserts; use returning({id}) to read it back. Reject any plan that proposes explicit randomUUID() in service code.

Wallet ledger balance_after_vc invariant: must be computed from wallet.balance_vc AFTER the row-locked UPDATE. For signup_bonus it equals SIGNUP_BONUS_VC only because wallet was 0 before. Generalises to all future writers (bet stake, top-up) — they need lock then compute then insert then update.

Drizzle vi.mock for service tests: stub the schema module with table objects carrying a _name marker, then route inserts/selects in the mock db by that name. Avoids loading the real schema chain (which imports from @mlabs/db/schema/auth and friends) while keeping the test small and table-aware.

Workspace-package QA scripts: when an .mstack/qa/<run>/ script imports @mlabs/*, Node resolves node_modules upward from the script dir — but .mstack/ has none. Copy the script into apps/web/scripts/ (or any package dir) before running. Or write the script in plain DB/SQL calls and skip the service layer.

Stripe webhook idempotency at two layers: WebhookEvent table (UNIQUE on event.id, onConflictDoNothing) catches re-deliveries; service-side credit check (existing wallet_transaction.reference_id = paymentTransactionId) is belt-and-braces. Don't rely on PaymentTransaction.status as the only dedupe key — a single PaymentTransaction can receive multiple events (session.completed + charge.succeeded).

Drizzle seed migrations: append INSERTs to the generated schema migration with ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING. One file, one journal entry, idempotent on re-runs. Avoids the complexity of separate hand-written .sql files which need manual _journal.json edits.

Wallet ledger writer helper: __creditWallet(tx, {userId, kind, deltaVC, source, description, referenceId, idempotencyCheck}) accepts the idempotency predicate as a callback. creditSignupBonus passes a kind-based check; creditTopUp passes a reference-based check. Both public functions stay short, the shared insert+balance-update flow lives in one place.

Services package that hardcodes the brand name in any string literal trips no-brand-string-literal hard. Add @mlabs/config workspace dep + import { brand } from '@mlabs/config' at the same time as the literal. The rule's allowlist (config/templates/legal/docs/tests) does NOT include packages/services/src/.
Server Action return shape: discriminated union { url: string } | { error: string }. Client narrows via , not  — the latter TS-errors because url isn't on the error branch.

Replit dev server + HTTPS BETTER_AUTH_URL fallback means __Secure- cookies — Playwright can't authenticate over HTTP loopback. Workaround for service-layer QA: DB smoke script (tsx + workspace imports from apps/web/scripts/, deleted after run) hits live DB through the real service functions. Browser-side authed page smoke stays manual until either dev server is restarted with HTTP BETTER_AUTH_URL or e2e fixture grows a Replit-aware mode.

Session-level Postgres advisory locks (pg_try_advisory_lock) + PgBouncer + ephemeral processes = stale-lock footgun. When the process crashes before pg_advisory_unlock, the pooler keeps the backend session alive and the lock is held indefinitely. Prefer transactional locks (pg_advisory_xact_lock) or no lock at all — only add a session-level lock if you've confirmed direct (non-pooled) connections and clean SIGTERM handling. Source: BetFrnd build 1e14cd6f failure, 2026-05-17.

Before adding a concurrency guard (advisory lock, mutex table, leader election), verify the race actually exists in your deployment model. Replit Reserved VM serialises deploys per app — our migrate-lock was guarding against a race that the platform already prevents, and the guard itself became the bug. Default to trusting the deploy serialisation when known; document the assumption so future devs can validate it.

When removing a load-bearing concurrency guard (advisory lock, mutex, etc.), don't just delete the corresponding Tier 3 'do not touch' doc entry — replace it with a warning describing the *assumption* the lock-free design now depends on. The assumption becomes the new load-bearing thing; future devs/forks need to know what to revisit if they change the deployment topology that makes the assumption hold.

When a /mlabs-review acceptance criterion uses a strict grep over a file, but the same task requires adding a rationale comment that necessarily mentions the removed concept, the criterion is self-contradictory. Resolve by: (1) running both the strict and code-only greps, (2) confirming the spirit (no executable code references) is met, (3) recording the accept-as-intentional decision in log.md. Don't blindly fail acceptance on a contradiction between two parts of the same task spec.

BetFrnd repo has a pre-existing turbo circular build-graph edge between @mlabs/db and @mlabs/services. Monorepo-wide `pnpm typecheck` aborts before TS runs. Per-package typechecks pass. When verifying TS validity in this repo, prefer `pnpm --filter @mlabs/<pkg> typecheck` over the monorepo-wide command until the cycle is fixed.

Adding a new admin mutation requires extending the AuditMeta typed union in packages/db/src/audit.ts — the audit_log.metadata jsonb column looks free-form but the type is a strict discriminated union by design (GDPR anonymize-in-place safety). Easy to miss when reusing audit() in a new domain.

Stripe Checkout in a react-native-webview: don't render the success/cancel URLs inside the WebView. Use onShouldStartLoadWithRequest to intercept navigation to those sentinel URLs, return false, and trigger a native banner + pop instead. Same webhook flow as web; no auth-cookie sharing into the WebView.

Service handlers in this codebase take (db, ctx: CallerContext, args). adminId/userId come from ctx.userId (set by defineOperation's auth boundary), never from args. Plans that put auth-identifiers in args bypass the convention and the type system can't catch it because typescript will accept the shape.

Existing admin services write audit_log BEFORE the mutation, but NOT inside the mutation's db.transaction. This means a failed mutation leaves an orphan audit row. createAudit(db) closes over the outer db connection — it can't accept a tx without a refactor of every caller. Plans that claim 'audit inside the transaction' for new admin actions need to either match the existing looser pattern or explicitly budget the refactor.

HTML5 native <dialog> element with showModal() works well in this codebase when ui-web lacks a Dialog primitive — zero new deps, ::backdrop styling via Tailwind, browser-built focus trap + esc-to-close. dialogRef.current?.showModal() / .close(). See features/admin/components/wallet-adjust-form.tsx.

wallet/service.ts grew an ApiError import (from @mlabs/api) for the first time. Pre-existing wallet writers throw plain Errors; admin-facing methods (creditAdminAdjust) need typed codes for the admin UI. Pattern: any wallet writer that can fail in a user-actionable way should throw ApiError; pure internal invariants stay as plain Error.

Replit-aware e2e fixture invocation: run with BETTER_AUTH_URL=https://$REPLIT_DEV_DOMAIN (HTTPS) so auth.handler signs a __Secure-better-auth.session_token cookie that matches the running dev server's expectations. globalSetup hardcodes domain:127.0.0.1 + captures the actual secure flag from Set-Cookie — when called via http auth.handler returns secure:false, letting the browser send the cookie to http://127.0.0.1:5000. Bypasses the TODOS.md 'E2E auth fixture — Replit-aware mode' blocker without code changes to the fixture.

Mobile env wrapper module (lib/config/env.ts) does NOT exist and is not idiomatic in this Expo codebase. Use process.env.EXPO_PUBLIC_* references inline at call sites — Metro replaces them at build time. Web's @/config/env Zod-validated module is server-side; mobile has no analogue and shouldn't get one until env count grows significantly.

No rate-limit infra exists in this codebase (no lib/ratelimit.ts, no Upstash dep). Plans that casually say 'throttled to N per minute per user' need either (a) a new infra task budgeted up front or (b) explicit defer. The 2b polish bundle already lists rate-limit-startCheckout; add new rate-limit needs there until V1 ships.

Mobile API routes follow the apps/web/src/app/api/v1/notifications/unread-count/route.ts shape — manual getSessionFromHeaders + buildContext + service call + NextResponse.json. NOT defineOperation. Plans claiming 'wraps the X service' need to verify whether X is a service (in @mlabs/services) or a Server Action (in features/*/server/actions.ts); mobile routes call services directly, never Server Actions.

react-native-webview sentinel-URL intercept: open Stripe Checkout (or any auth-gated callback) and return false from onShouldStartLoadWithRequest when the URL matches your success/cancel sentinel. Triggers a native navigation + refetch instead of loading the URL in the WebView. No auth cookie ever enters the WebView; the webhook credits the wallet independently via payment_transaction.id stored in Stripe session metadata. See apps/mobile/app/(app)/wallet/checkout.tsx + TEMPLATE.md lesson #29.

vi.mock factories are hoisted to the top of the test file — they can't reference values declared with class/const below them. Use vi.hoisted(() => { class …; const fn = vi.fn(); return { … } }) to declare mock implementations that the factory needs. Pattern used in apps/web/tests/wallet-checkout-route.test.ts.

Async useEffect polling loops in React Native need every nested setTimeout tracked + a cancelled-flag guard around await points. Returning a cleanup that only clears the initial timer leaks subsequent ticks; if the user navigates away mid-poll, setState fires on an unmounted screen → React warning. Pattern: Set<TimeoutID> + let cancelled = false + check cancelled before/after each await. See apps/mobile/app/(app)/wallet/index.tsx success-banner effect.

When evaluating a SaaS provider, the docs intro page and the marketing pricing page may disagree about quotas. odds-api.io's docs claimed '5,000 req/hr on all plans' but the Free tier is actually 100/hr (confirmed empirically via x-ratelimit-limit headers). Verify the real ceiling via response headers BEFORE locking polling cadence in a plan.

Plans citing existing patterns must verify the cited file, not the cited memory. The sports-3a plan claimed 'no CHECK on kind, same as wallet' but wallet.ts:86-89 actually has a CHECK constraint on wallet_transaction.kind. Always Read the precedent file before treating a plan's 'matches existing pattern' claim as accurate.

odds-api.io tennis tournament slugs are ephemeral — each tournament (e.g. atp-french-open-men-singles, wta-wta-rabat-morocco-women-singles) gets its own slug that lives ONLY while the tournament is active. No umbrella 'atp-tour' / 'wta-tour' slug exists. Allowlists encoded in code go stale within weeks; admin UI must handle this in Sprint 3b. Football slugs are stable (england-premier-league etc.); tennis is the exception.

Raw SQL INSERT into a Drizzle-managed table with id text $defaultFn(crypto.randomUUID()) WILL fail on NOT NULL before any other constraint (CHECK, FK) is evaluated. The defaultFn only fires inside the Drizzle insert builder. For raw-SQL DB tests, use gen_random_uuid() or another server-side default in the SQL itself. Also assert SQLSTATE codes (23514 = check_violation, 23502 = not_null_violation) so a wrong-reason pass doesn't fool you into thinking your CHECK constraint works.

Next.js 16's instrumentation hook (apps/web/src/instrumentation.ts) is the right place to boot long-lived worker pools (pg-boss, BullMQ, etc.). The hook fires once per server start before the first request. Pattern: thin trampoline in instrumentation.ts dynamically imports a worker module from packages/services and calls its start function. Singleton guard inside the worker module prevents double-boot on hot reload. SIGTERM handler in the worker calls graceful stop. Avoids the older 'boot lazily on first request' pattern which had race conditions and request-scoped lifetimes.

Before pinning a Node-side dep, check the registry for engines.node on the latest several majors — silent Node-version creep is real. pg-boss bumped its minimum from Node 16 (v9) → 20 (v10) → 22 (v11+) within a year. The Replit deploy pins Node 20.18.1; installing latest pg-boss would have silently shipped a broken deploy. Verified via 'node -e' against registry.npmjs.org rather than trusting the docs. Apply this check to any worker/runtime dep introduction.

pg-boss v10 dropped the teamConcurrency work option that v9 had — concurrency is now controlled at queue-creation time via queue policies (standard/short/singleton/stately). For schedule-driven crons this doesn't matter: pg-boss serializes jobs via SKIP LOCKED at the queue layer. So boss.work(name, handler) without options is correct in v10. If you mechanically copy v9-era examples (which exist all over the internet), the TypeScript types in WorkOptions reject teamConcurrency cleanly — good signal vs silently doing nothing.

pg-boss v10 enforces a foreign key from pgboss.schedule.name to pgboss.queue.name. v9 implicitly created queues on first send/schedule; v10 requires an explicit boss.createQueue(name) before boss.schedule(name, ...). Tests with mocked db won't catch this — only real Postgres surfaces the FK violation (SQLSTATE 23503). When introducing a queue dep, always do an end-to-end first boot against the real DB before declaring the worker done.

Read-only UI sprints are valuable as a separate ship between data layer and interaction layer. Sprint 3c is read-only fixture browse — no bet slip, no interactions on the odds. Letting users see the fixtures before they can place bets gives QA + the team something concrete to test the cron pipeline against, surfaces UX issues with sport tabs / league filter / date filter, and keeps the bet placement sprint (Sprint 4) tightly focused on the placement logic itself. Lock 'no interactions in 3c' explicitly during planning so the team doesn't drift into 'might as well wire up the slip' creep.

Server-component helpers like requireUser() throw or redirect — wrong for JSON REST routes. v1 mobile API routes use getSessionFromHeaders(req.headers) + manual ApiError.unauthorized().toResponse() so unauth returns 401 JSON, not an HTML 302. Always check the auth helper's failure mode (throw vs redirect vs return null) against the route's expected response shape. When planning a new REST surface, point at the closest precedent (v1 wallet routes for mobile JSON, server actions for form posts, server components for page rendering).

expo-router v55 supports  on Tabs.Screen to hide a tab from the bar while keeping the route live for Link navigation + push deep links. Implementation: sets tabBarItemStyle.display='none' and replaces tabBarButton with a null-returning component. Verified in node_modules/expo-router/build/layouts/TabsClient.js. Use this pattern when restructuring tabs without breaking existing deep links — better than deleting Tabs.Screen entries and rebuilding the route from scratch.

BetterAuth's cookie verification depends on the baseUrl matching between cookie-issuance and cookie-verification time. apps/web/src/lib/auth/index.ts falls back from BETTER_AUTH_URL to https://REPLIT_DEV_DOMAIN. On Replit, default dev launcher leaves BETTER_AUTH_URL unset → server uses the Replit dev URL. globalSetup (e2e fixture) runs with BETTER_AUTH_URL=http://127.0.0.1:5000 → issues a cookie under that baseUrl. Server silently rejects (200 + null from /api/auth/get-session). Pin BETTER_AUTH_URL on BOTH dev server AND globalSetup. Tracked in .replit workflow line for the project's pnpm dev.

Pinning BETTER_AUTH_URL on the default Replit dev workflow has a side effect: browser sessions on the Replit dev domain see BetterAuth issuing cookies/redirects/email-verify links pointing at 127.0.0.1 instead of the dev domain (because apps/web/src/lib/auth/index.ts:17 prefers BETTER_AUTH_URL over the REPLIT_DEV_DOMAIN fallback). Fix: split the workflow. Default "Start application" leaves BETTER_AUTH_URL unset so REPLIT_DEV_DOMAIN wins; a separate "Start application (e2e)" re-pins http://127.0.0.1:5000 only when running QA-style Playwright suites that reuse :5000 (e.g. .mstack/qa/*/playwright.config.ts with no webServer block). Standard pnpm e2e uses port 3000 with its own webServer and is unaffected either way.

Bet placement requires SELECT FOR UPDATE in three places inside a single Postgres transaction: (1) wallet row (balance read), (2) bet count for the cap check, (3) each event row referenced by a leg (verify pending + start_time > now). All three locks taken together — Postgres serialises any concurrent placement that conflicts. Simpler than advisory locks or optimistic-retry, and reuses the Sprint 2a wallet __creditWallet pattern for the actual stake debit. Pattern for any future write that depends on multi-row invariants (e.g. signal purchase, seller eligibility) in this codebase.

Postgres rejects SELECT ... FOR UPDATE on aggregate queries (count, sum, etc.) — error: 'FOR UPDATE is not allowed with aggregate functions'. Drizzle's typed builder doesn't catch this; the SQL is rejected at runtime. When you need 'no concurrent inserts can change a count,' lock a row that gates the action instead — for bet placement, locking the user's wallet row (UNIQUE(user_id)) serialises all placements for that user via UNIQUE constraint contention. Subsequent reads see the committed state via MVCC. Pattern: lock a UNIQUE-constrained row that's always part of the write, then read the count plain (no FOR UPDATE).

Two-layer test convention: unit-cover pre-txn validation paths; defer in-transaction + raw SQL paths to /mlabs-qa via describe.skip blocks. Established Sprint 3c, reaffirmed Sprint 4a. Visible in pnpm test output without faking SELECT FOR UPDATE / DISTINCT ON semantics.

Replit Agent IDE auto-checkpoints can absorb in-progress task edits into a generic 'Saved your changes before starting work' commit. T12 (/bets/open page + redirect) landed as auto-commit 4784177, bundling unrelated state and a parallel-session plan file from another Replit user. Source files are in git and correct, but the atomic-commit-per-task contract is broken by IDE behaviour. Mitigation: document in implementation report; do not attempt rebase (would risk losing other user's parallel work).

Bet placement concurrency: lock UNIQUE(user_id) wallet row with SELECT FOR UPDATE; the unique index serialises placements per user. Concurrent count(*) for 5-bet cap uses MVCC reads (no FOR UPDATE — Postgres rejects FOR UPDATE on aggregate queries). Stake debit + cap check + per-leg event timing all share the wallet-row lock window.

Playwright specs (vs globalSetup) use Node ESM runtime resolution, not Bundler-mode TS resolution. Importing @mlabs/db/schema from a spec fails on the package-internal 'from "./auth"' extensionless imports. Workaround: raw Pool+SQL via @neondatabase/serverless. Documented in apps/web/e2e/support/auth.ts.

QA bearer-header auth fallback: when cookie-based auth fights URL-domain/Secure/sameSite issues, the bearer() plugin in BetterAuth accepts the same token via Authorization header. Cookie SIGNATURE is HMAC over secret + session-id only — not URL-bound — so the same token works either transport. Used in .mstack/qa/2026-05-21-0547/specs/bets-4a-placement.authed.spec.ts and stayed even after the cookie-domain parameterization fix as belt-and-braces.

/mlabs-qa two-layer pattern for state-machine features (placement, settlement, payouts): (1) service-level integration runner against dev Neon for in-transaction paths — captures 30 assertions for 9 placeBet scenarios in ~3s, deterministic. (2) Single Playwright happy-path spec for end-to-end UI wiring + screenshots. Skip 9 separate Playwright specs when service-level coverage is the real value.

Sprint 4b settlement service exposes a postSettlementHook(tx, userId, betId, outcome) optional callback inside the same Postgres transaction. Locked because Sprint 5a (signals-eligibility-foundation, plan 2026-05-21-signals-5a-eligibility-foundation.md) has a hard precondition on this seam to atomically update user_stats. Default no-op so 4b ships standalone.

event_result.home_score/away_score is authoritative for BOTH football and tennis match-winner outcomes (per docs/handover/odds-api-io.md §5.5 line 314). Sprint 4b's resolveLegOutcome reads it uniformly — no sport branch for the score comparison. Only the draw-outcome key is sport-specific (football allows it, tennis returns void on tie).

settleBet(db, args, hooks?) has NO ctx parameter — settlement is system-initiated (cron-fired), no user request, no audit-log actor. Diverges from placeBet(db, ctx, input) but matches Sprint 5a's recomputeUserStatsOnSettlement(tx, ...) precedent. Hook signature: (tx, userId, betId, outcome) => Promise<void>.

Sprint 4b drops the bet.settlement_outcome column from the plan — bet.status IN ('won','lost','void') after settlement carries the same info. Sprint 5a's postSettlementHook receives outcome as a parameter from settleBet directly, so the column isn't load-bearing for downstream consumers either.

Two-layer test convention reaffirmed at Sprint 4b — unit-test dispatch/validation paths in the service test file, defer in-transaction success paths to /mlabs-qa via describe.skip block enumerating scenario names. Established at 3c, 4a, now 4b. Worth lifting to a CLAUDE.md section.

Drizzle's execute<T>() requires T extend Record<string,unknown> (i.e. open index signature). Strict typed interfaces fail with 'Index signature for type string is missing'. Fix: use a type alias with  index, not an interface. Hit at packages/services/src/bets/settlement.ts when typing the legRows JOIN.

Sprint 4b kept the existing bet.status CHECK constraint that allows 'settling' even though settleBet never writes that value (review locked: skip the SETTLING intermediate state, go OPEN → WON/LOST/VOID directly). The constraint is too cheap to migrate and removing 'settling' would be destructive. Status values not used don't hurt anyone.

Drizzle's execute<T>() requires T extend Record<string,unknown> (i.e. open index signature). Strict typed interfaces fail with 'Index signature for type string is missing'. Fix: use a type alias with an open index signature (key: string -> unknown), not an interface. Hit at packages/services/src/bets/settlement.ts when typing the legRows JOIN.

Payout math in JS: floor(stake * product(odds)) with raw floats gives off-by-1 VC errors. 1.5*2.0*1.4 = 4.199999999999999, not 4.2; floor(100 * that) = 419 instead of 420. Fix: round the product to numeric column precision (4dp matches captured_odds_decimal) BEFORE the final floor. Shared helper at packages/services/src/bets/payout.ts now used by both placeBet and settleBet.

Integration runner pattern for settleBet (settlement) parallels the placeBet runner (placement). Insert synthetic bet + bet_leg rows under a dedicated qa-* user, attach to real settled events (74 in dev), force WON/LOST/VOID by choosing outcome_key vs winner_key OR by nulling event_result.home_score temporarily. Cleanup via DELETE user (cascade). Pattern reusable for settlement-adjacent features (e.g. user_stats from 5a).

@mlabs/services/bets/payout subpath was added in Sprint 4b QA fix 7fe34bf for the web client to use without dragging server-only deps. The same subpath is now also load-bearing for mobile (Sprint 4c) because computePayoutVc + computeOddsTotalAt4dp are pure number math (no DB, no async, no server-only). Pattern: when a math helper is needed by BOTH server (write) AND client (display), give it its own subpath in @mlabs/services/<domain>/<helper> and ensure no server-only imports leak in. One source of truth for placement math across web/mobile/server.

Sprint 4c locks MAX_LEGS re-export from @mlabs/services/bets/payout (re-exports MAX_LEGS_PER_BET from constants). Both web slip-context and mobile slip-context import the cap from the same subpath. Kills the 3-place drift risk identified in plan review.

Mobile REST routes for bets follow the existing v1 thin-adapter convention (apps/web/src/app/api/v1/wallet/route.ts precedent): runtime='nodejs', session via getSessionFromHeaders, direct service call, ApiError.toResponse() for errors. NOT defineOperation — that's for the web Server Actions layer. List responses wrap in { bets: [...] }; single responses return the schema raw. Lock applies to all v1 mobile routes going forward.

Sprint 4c approves @gorhom/bottom-sheet@5.x as a new top-level mobile dep. Peer deps (react-native-gesture-handler 2.30.1, react-native-reanimated 4.2.1) confirmed already installed via Expo SDK 55. Sheet UX is the de-facto RN convention; hand-rolling Reanimated gesture code (~200 LoC) for keyboard avoidance + backdrop + pull-down dismissed as not worth the maintenance. Lock at v5.x for Reanimated 4 compat.

Sprint 4c established the cross-platform pattern for client-safe math helpers: @mlabs/services/<domain>/<helper> subpath with NO server-only imports + NO Drizzle imports. Both web client (apps/web/.../bet-slip/page.tsx) and mobile (apps/mobile/features/bets/components/slip-sheet.tsx) import computePayoutVc + computeOddsTotalAt4dp from the same package path, ensuring displayed values match what server placement + server settlement actually compute. Re-export pattern (MAX_LEGS_PER_BET as MAX_LEGS) lets the canonical name stay in constants.ts while the subpath surfaces a short name.

@gorhom/bottom-sheet v5 exports BottomSheetModal as BOTH a default AND a named export. Using `import BottomSheetModal, { ... }` triggers eslint's import/no-named-as-default. Fix: use the named import only — `import { BottomSheetModal, ... }`. TypeScript type alias works via `type BottomSheetModal as BottomSheetModalType` in the same named import.

Mobile slip UX uses a bottom-sheet drawer (@gorhom/bottom-sheet) over a dedicated /bet-slip route. Snap points 40/85%. Reasoning: keeps browse context when reviewing selections (industry-standard pattern from DraftKings, bet365 mobile, FanDuel). The web side keeps its /bet-slip page; the patterns diverge intentionally — web has more screen real estate to spare on a full page.

Cross-session collision pattern observed in Sprint 4c pre-flight: parallel mlabs-code session was running 5a while my mlabs-plan/mlabs-review for 4c finished. Their git add caught my untracked 4c plan + review + 3 learnings into commit 482840e (accidentally). Their session shipped 5a cleanly; my files came through with it. Lesson: when running multiple mstack sessions in parallel, isolate work to separate feature branches BEFORE the plan/review docs land, OR coordinate on which session lands ledger files to main first.

ApiError.toResponse() returns the locked OV7 wire shape: { error: { code, message, field? } }. NOT a flat { code, message }. Sprint 4c QA runner initially read body.code (returned undefined) instead of body.error.code. Lock the body.error.code path in all future REST-route QA scripts. Source: packages/api/src/errors.ts:40-46.

Mobile QA strategy when no device/sim is available: verify the REST wire surface end-to-end (auth gating, input validation codes, service error propagation, pagination, response shape) as Layer A; document Maestro flow + run command for the user to execute on a real device as Layer B. Sprint 4c run shipped clean on Layer A only — sufficient confidence for ship even without a device-driven smoke.

Sprint 5b stubs Stripe Connect onboarding via a per-user boolean user.stripe_connect_onboarded (default false). createSignal short-circuits with signals.connect_not_onboarded if false. Sprint 6 fills in the real OAuth flow + flips the flag via webhook. Pattern: when a hard precondition needs another sprint's work, plant the column + the gate + a 'coming in Sprint X' UI placeholder NOW so the dependent sprint has clean seams to fill.

Sprint 5b's createSignal needs an explicit pre-txn check for signal_for_bet_exists even though signal.bet_id is UNIQUE. The 5a UNIQUE constraint catches the race but emits a raw Postgres 23505 to the caller. A friendlier code (signals.signal_for_bet_exists) lets the UI explain 'a signal was already created from this bet' instead of 'database error'. Pattern: when a DB UNIQUE column is reachable from a user-facing service, always add an explicit pre-check + ApiError code; UNIQUE stays as defense-in-depth for concurrent writes.

Auto-close crons should be purely time-based (status='active' AND close_buy_at <= now()). Coupling to downstream state (e.g. underlying bet has settled) conflates two distinct close reasons into status='closed' and makes the cron query JOIN against state that changes asynchronously. Sprint 5b's signal-close locks the pattern: time is the close trigger; state-driven blocks happen at access-time (Sprint 6 purchase flow). Same shape as Sprint 4b's bet-settle-sweep — single-condition WHERE clause + RETURNING for observability.

Sprint 5b grows the sidebar to 6 items by adding /seller between Wallet and Signals. /seller is the private seller dashboard (5b); /signals is the public marketplace (5c, currently a ComingSoon stub). Two distinct surfaces, two distinct nav items. Reviewer-locked over the 'replace /signals temporarily' alternative — the two-sided product structure deserves two persistent nav slots.

Drizzle mock-tx chains in unit tests need a thenable on .where() when the service awaits .where() directly (no .limit() suffix — common for multi-row reads like bet_leg). Add { then: (resolve) => resolve(rows) } alongside { limit, for } on the where() return. Used in packages/services/src/signals/__tests__/service.test.ts; same pattern usable for any service mocking multi-row Drizzle queries.

Sprint 5b confirmed the Sprint-N-stubs-Sprint-(N+1)-gate pattern: 5b plants user.stripe_connect_onboarded (boolean DEFAULT false) + the createSignal gate + a 'Connect Stripe (Sprint 6)' disabled CTA placeholder; Sprint 6 flips the flag via real OAuth/webhook. Locks the dependent sprint's surface area BEFORE the dependent work lands. Companion: scripts/dev-flip-<feature>.ts for QA toggle until the real seam exists.

Sprint 5b's createSignal pre-checks signal.bet_id BEFORE the UNIQUE constraint can fire — emits friendlier signals.signal_for_bet_exists code instead of raw Postgres 23505. The UNIQUE constraint stays as defense-in-depth for concurrent-create races. Pattern: when a DB UNIQUE column is reachable from a user-facing service, always add an explicit pre-check + ApiError code; UNIQUE stays defense-in-depth.

Postgres SELECT FOR UPDATE only locks rows that MATCH the WHERE clause. When zero rows match (e.g. 'check the seller's existing active signal' with none yet), no lock is acquired at all. Concurrent transactions both see 'no row' and proceed — the FOR UPDATE provides zero protection for the no-row case. For 'at most one row matching predicate' invariants, use a partial UNIQUE INDEX + try/catch on INSERT to translate 23505 to a friendly ApiError. Caught at Sprint 5b QA (createSignal one-active-per-seller race).

Sprint 5b established the QA pattern for concurrency-sensitive services: run two createSignal calls via Promise.allSettled([...]) and assert exactly one fulfilled + one rejected with the expected ApiError code. Tests the truly-concurrent path (different Postgres connections, real row-locking) that mock-tx unit tests can't reach. Same shape reusable for any service guarding 'at most one X per Y'.

Drizzle wraps Postgres errors in DrizzleQueryError; the underlying NeonDbError (with .code SQLSTATE and .constraint name) lives in .cause. Helper pattern: walk the error chain (function unwrapPostgresError(err) → recurse on err.cause until shape.code is a string). Used in packages/services/src/signals/service.ts to dispatch 23505 to the right ApiError by constraint name.

Sprint 5c locks the marketplace UX: pre-purchase legs are redacted to event-label + kickoff-time only; outcome_key + captured_odds_decimal absent from the wire (knowing captured odds would let buyers reverse-engineer the pick — high odds favours underdog, etc.). PRD §E.15 'legs are redacted/locked' interpreted as 'pick redacted but event metadata visible'. Future Sprint 6 unlocked-state branch adds the full leg shape when viewerHasPurchased=true.

Correlated subqueries with LIMIT 1 need an explicit ORDER BY for deterministic results. Sprint 5c's listSignalsForBrowse derives an accumulator's sport from its first leg via a correlated subquery; without ORDER BY start_time_utc ASC LIMIT 1, the row chosen is non-deterministic across query runs and the sport filter would be unstable. Lock the ORDER BY in all such patterns.

Cross-feature imports of KickoffCountdown (features/sports → features/bets in 5b → features/signals in 5c) is now an established one-off pattern. When a 3rd feature needs the same component, lift to a shared components/common/ then. Re-evaluate at the start of every feature sprint that adds a major UX surface.

Sprint 5c plants the viewerHasPurchased seam in getSignalForDetail(db, id, { viewerHasPurchased?: boolean }) — the parameter is accepted but unused in 5c (always returns redacted legs). Sprint 6 will flip it when the SignalPurchase check returns true. Pattern: when a sprint's UI/service is the read-side of a flow that another sprint owns the write-side of, plant the seam at the right shape NOW so the dependent sprint doesn't need a service-signature refactor.

When two atomic tasks have a typecheck-graph dependency (e.g. T11 route screen imports from T12 barrel that re-exports T11's siblings), splitting them into separate commits creates an intermediate state where typecheck fails. Bundle them into one commit and document in the commit message. Caught at Sprint 5c T11+T12 — route screens couldn't typecheck without the barrel re-exports already in place.

Service + validator ordering: when a service file imports types from validators, land validators first regardless of plan order. Sprint 5c swapped T1 (service) and T2 (validators) commit order to keep typecheck green across the transition. The plan's logical order is OK; the commit order needs to follow the dependency graph.

Sprint 5c mobile detail screen ships with last10=[] (empty W/L/V dots). The web SSR call to getLast10SettledBetOutcomes can't be made from a client mobile screen — no public 'GET /api/v1/sellers/[id]/last10' REST surface exists. Two follow-up options: (a) add the REST route, (b) include last10Outcomes in getSignalForDetail's response (one extra LIMIT 10 query inside the existing function). Defer to Sprint 7 polish.

db.execute(sql`...`) on @neondatabase/serverless WebSocket Pool returns timestamptz as a Postgres-text string, NOT a Date. Drizzle's typed select().from(table) DOES parse to Date because the schema drives column parsing. When using raw execute<>() for join+subquery+window queries, declare timestamp columns as 'Date | string' and normalize to ISO via new Date(v).toISOString() (or your own toIso() helper). Mock-tx unit tests cannot catch this — they pass Date stubs, hiding the lie. Always integration-test raw-execute queries against real Neon. Caught in Sprint 5c QA, fix commit c3db1d4.

When the review locks 'detail mirrors browse' for filter parity, write the filter at the service boundary, not the route handler. Sprint 5c's getSignalForDetail dropped close_buy_at>now() while listSignalsForBrowse kept it; the deferred integration test (queries.test.ts:282 stub) caught it but the mock-tx unit tests couldn't. Pattern: deferred test stubs that name a specific spec invariant ARE the spec — wire them into the integration runner before marking the sprint shipped. Fix commit bb96652.

Mobile auth-gate placement: gate must live on (app)/_layout.tsx, NOT on (auth)/_layout.tsx or root, because (auth)/verify?token=... and (auth)/reset-password?token=... are hit by signed-out users from email deep links. Group-level gates that block signed-out access would brick email-driven flows. Same applies to any future web app deep-link routes.

Mobile API_BASE_URL defaults to http://localhost:3000 (apps/mobile/lib/api/client.ts:22-24), but the web dev server runs on port 5000 (CLAUDE.md, package scripts). On Expo web preview at 8080, useMe() therefore always errors against the wrong port — gates that branch on auth error will always fire signed-out. To validate signed-in paths on web preview, set EXPO_PUBLIC_API_BASE_URL=http://<host>:5000 (also requires CORS + SecureStore-on-web handling). For QA, expect to validate signed-in flows on Expo Go / simulator instead.

Replit cross-port CORS for Expo web preview: when Expo serves on :8080 and Next.js API on :5000 (mapped to externalPort 80 via .replit), they're same-domain-different-origin and the browser CORS-preflights. Bearer-token auth bypasses cookie/CSRF concerns but not CORS. Fix: extend the auth factory to accept trustedOrigins, derive from REPLIT_DEV_DOMAIN with both bare and ':8080' variants. SecureStore on web is a known no-op / in-memory fallback — sessions drop on hard refresh; document don't fix.

BetterAuth's trustedOrigins is CSRF-only — it does NOT set Access-Control-Allow-* response headers. The catch-all auth route (toNextJsHandler) only exports GET/POST, so preflight OPTIONS falls through to Next.js's default and the browser rejects the request. For cross-origin browser fetches to /api/auth/* (Expo web on :8080 -> Next.js on bare domain) add a Next.js middleware.ts matching /api/auth/:path*, validate Origin against the trustedOrigins list, short-circuit OPTIONS with 204+CORS headers, decorate GET/POST responses with Allow-Origin+Allow-Credentials+Vary. Native/EAS builds skip CORS so middleware is a no-op when REPLIT_DEV_DOMAIN is unset.

expo-secure-store v55's web build is  — calling SecureStore.getItemAsync/setItemAsync/deleteItemAsync on web throws 'TypeError: ExpoSecureStore.default.getValueWithKeyAsync is not a function'. Any mobile feature that authenticates needs a Platform.OS === 'web' branch that routes to localStorage (or sessionStorage). For BetFrnd the shim lives in apps/mobile/lib/api/client.ts in tokenStore. CORS Allow-Headers also need to include every header the mobile buildHeaders sets (X-Client at minimum; also Accept, If-Modified-Since) or the browser blocks preflight with 'Request header field x-client is not allowed'.

Playwright + RN-web TextInput: .fill() can set DOM value without firing onChangeText (React state stays empty -> validation rejects). Use locator.click() then locator.pressSequentially(text, {delay: 10}) to type chars one-at-a-time, triggering each onChange. Verify via locator.inputValue() before submit if the test is fragile.

Sprint 6c: added explicit paid_at column to signal_purchase instead of relying on updated_at. updated_at is fragile against future paths (refund flow, retries) that bump it for non-payment reasons. Rule of thumb: state-machine transitions deserve explicit timestamps, not implicit updated_at semantics.

Review-side correction: plan authors should grep the codebase for the actual file path of any service the plan claims to edit. Sprint 6c plan said webhook handler was at packages/services/src/purchases/webhook-handler.ts; actual is packages/services/src/billing/webhook.ts. Cheap to mis-state, cheap to verify before review locks.

drizzle-orm vi.mock that stubs sql as () => ({_op:'sql'}) doesn't preserve tagged-template params. Tests that try to assert which user_id/limit got threaded through fail because the mock returns the same opaque object regardless of bound values. Workaround: assert DTO-mapping behaviour instead, or upgrade the mock to capture template chunks. Sprint 6c Task 3 surfaced this.

Atomic-commit splitting when functions share scaffolding: write all functions + tests together for typecheck/test correctness, then revert the later-task additions, commit Task N, re-add Task N+1, commit, etc. Same final tree, atomic reviewable commits. Sprint 6c Tasks 3-5 used this when listPurchasesForBuyer + listEarningsForSeller + getEarningsSummaryForSeller shared PurchaseRowDto + mapPurchaseRow scaffolding.

Replit dev domain Playwright auth: globalSetup writes the session cookie scoped to E2E_BASE_URL (not BETTER_AUTH_URL); always pass E2E_BASE_URL=https://$REPLIT_DEV_DOMAIN to the playwright run so the cookie domain matches the live Replit URL. Without it, cookie domain defaults to 127.0.0.1 and pages return the login screen.

Replit globalSetup user-CASCADE cleanup fails when prior runs leave FK-referencing rows. Before each QA run, manually clean signal_purchase + signal + bet_leg + bet rows for usernames like 'e2e-test-%' (FK ON DELETE is RESTRICT, not CASCADE). Same gotcha hit by Sprint 6c QA + design-review 2026-05-22-1745.

Sprint 6d.1: kept stripe_connect_account_id on account.application.deauthorized (only flipping onboarded=false), not NULLing it. Audit-safe — signal_purchase.stripe_transfer_id chains back through the original Connect account. Re-onboarding via existing /seller/connect handles the 'use existing account ID' case via Stripe accountLinks.create({account: id}).

Two pre-existing token violations surfaced during 6d.1 review: /admin/sports/jobs StatePill uses raw Tailwind colors (bg-green-500/10 etc.) not brand tokens. Don't copy this pattern; the /admin/payouts page in 6d.1 uses brand tokens. Capture sports/jobs cleanup as a follow-up TODO.

Sprint 6a's startConnectOnboarding already supports re-onboarding with an existing stripe_connect_account_id (apps/web/src/features/connect/server/actions.ts — ). Means audit-safe deauthorize (keep account ID, flip onboarded=false only) is the supported path, not a hack. Verified during 6d.1 review.

vi.spyOn on a same-module function does not intercept internal calls under ES modules. sweepFailedTransfers calls attemptTransfer via a direct binding established at parse time; spying via vi.spyOn(transferRetry, 'attemptTransfer') reassigns the namespace property but the internal caller is bound to the original. Workarounds: vi.mock the module to intercept the import, OR test the function as a black box (input rows → output counters).

vi.hoisted is the vitest escape hatch for referencing module-scope spies inside a vi.mock factory (which is hoisted). Pattern: const { spy } = vi.hoisted(() => ({ spy: vi.fn(...) })); vi.mock('foo', () => ({ thing: spy })). Used in Sprint 6d.1 to mock the new attemptTransfer import from the webhook test.

QA helper namespacing: when seeding users in QA specs, namespace BOTH email AND username with a per-id suffix (e.g. last 8 chars of the UUID). Prior QA runs leave rows in dev DB; without namespacing, the second run hits user_email_unique / user_username_unique on UPSERT-by-id (the unique constraints fire on the OTHER columns even when ON CONFLICT (id) DO UPDATE is in place).

On a fresh clone where pnpm isn't pre-installed, 'corepack enable pnpm' fails with EACCES because it tries to symlink into /usr/local/bin. Workaround: 'corepack enable --install-directory $HOME/.local/bin pnpm' (provided $HOME/.local/bin is on PATH). Caught during 2026-05-23 template-hardening run on a Conductor workspace.

Test mock with circular self-reference (e.g. 'const db = { transaction: (cb) => cb(db) }') breaks under TS strict mode — db ends up implicit-any. Fix: 'const db: any = {}; Object.assign(db, { ... })'. Used during 2026-05-23 template-hardening for the .batch() → .transaction() mock conversion in admin/__tests__/service.test.ts.

Next.js 16's TypeScript-config compiler emits a .compiled.js with CommonJS exports = … when next.config.ts uses import.meta.url + __dirname rebinds (e.g. for outputFileTracingRoot in pnpm workspaces). With apps/web/package.json '"type": "module"', the .js output gets treated as ESM and Node throws 'ReferenceError: exports is not defined in ES module scope' on both pnpm dev and pnpm build. Fix: author next.config as .mjs instead of .ts (preserve typing via JSDoc /** @type {import("next").NextConfig} */). Caught during 2026-05-23 template-hardening /mlabs-qa run; fix at commit a5b882d.

scripts/rename.ts's TEXT_EXTENSIONS allowlist silently drops files whose extensions aren't listed — even when shouldRewrite() would otherwise rewrite them. .mstack/learnings.jsonl is the only .jsonl consumer and carries compound memory across forks, so missing .jsonl from TEXT_EXTENSIONS leaves stale @<source-scope>/ references in the new fork. Add .jsonl to TEXT_EXTENSIONS (commit ef17fcf). Generalises: every new file extension that ships in the template (any .toml, .nix, .env-style file) needs an explicit decision about whether the rename script rewrites it. Audit when adding a new tracked file type.

Email clients (Gmail, Outlook, Yahoo) do NOT support oklch() in inline CSS — design tokens from @mlabs/config/design cannot be used directly in React Email templates. Add a parallel sRGB hex palette (brand.emailColors) and import that for email rendering.

apps/web/src/app/_dev/ has existing seed pages (messages/, notifications/, states/) marked for deletion before v1 ship. Any new dev preview route must check this directory for path collisions before picking a slug.

React Email renders interleave text nodes with HTML comments (e.g. 'expires in <!-- -->60<!-- --> minutes'). Assertions on rendered HTML for literal phrases will fail; assert on the plaintext render (@react-email/render(tree, { plainText: true })) instead.
JSX in .ts files won't compile. When renaming .ts -> .tsx, also update package.json 'exports' map to point at the new extension — pnpm/TS module resolution does not silently fall back.

MLabs internal packages that need React JSX (e.g. @mlabs/email after the react-email refactor) need 'jsx: react-jsx' + lib: ['ES2022','DOM'] + .tsx in 'include' on the package's local tsconfig — the shared base only configures TS.
react-email versions 0.x are deprecated as of 2026-05. Pin @react-email/components to ^1.x and @react-email/render to ^2.x to avoid pulling in EOL transitive deps.

Next.js App Router treats folders prefixed with _ as PRIVATE (excluded from routing). /app/_dev/* paths 404 silently. Use 'dev/' (no underscore) or route groups '(dev)/' if you need them not to affect URLs. mlabs-plan/review didn't catch this; mlabs-qa surfaced it on first navigation.

BetterAuth requires DATABASE_URL at runtime even with SKIP_ENV_VALIDATION=1 — the flag only gates the env validator, not query execution. Without a real DB URL, every auth Server Action (signup, password reset, login) returns 500 with an empty body. The Next.js dev log at apps/web/.next/dev/logs/next-development.log has the real stack trace.

When running /mlabs-qa against an already-running dev server, write a temp Playwright config at .mstack/qa/<run>/playwright.qa.config.ts that sets testDir to ./specs and omits webServer (so it reuses the existing server). The repo's own playwright.config.ts hardcodes testDir to ./e2e which doesn't accept --testDir override.

Template-placeholder identity and agency identity should be the SAME word. The original split (Muscat = product placeholder, MLabs = agency name) forced rename.ts to maintain two parallel matcher families and produced post-rename doc drift where \bMLabs\b matchers over-replaced agency-warranty prose. Consolidating to a single 'mlabs' brand collapses the mental model and the matcher complexity. See .mstack/plans/2026-05-24-brand-consolidation.md.

The \bMLabs\b matcher in rename.ts was the source of post-rename doc mangling (agency-attribution prose in HANDOVER/DESIGN/AGENTS got rewritten to client name). Dropping it entirely \u2014 keeping only a 'MLabs Template' phrase matcher for the capital-M case \u2014 cleanly separates 'template self-naming' (rewritten) from 'agency attribution' (preserved). No SKIP_PATH_SUFFIXES growth required. See .mstack/reviews/2026-05-24-brand-consolidation.md.

Expo Router session gates must discriminate 401 from transient network errors. A naive gate that redirects on any useMe() error will bounce session-having users to welcome whenever the API is unreachable (airplane mode, dev server down, cold-start race). meRequest() should throw a typed ApiError with status, and the gate should only redirect when status === 401. Caught while planning apps/mobile welcome + session gate (2026-05-24).

Anchored-literal rename matchers must consider ALL syntactic wrappers the token can appear in, not just the canonical source form. The 'mlabs-mobile' matcher was initially quote-anchored (catches \"mlabs-mobile\" in TypeScript) but missed the backticked \`mlabs-mobile\` form in CHANGELOG markdown. Solution: when the token itself is unique enough (no realistic collision), prefer bare-token replacement over quote-anchored. Caught only because the new completeness test in rename.test.ts grepped post-rename output for the literal token, not just the quoted form.

apps/mobile/features/auth/api.ts meRequest() uses raw fetch instead of the apiRequest() wrapper, so the global 401->refresh-once retry in lib/api/client.ts is bypassed for the session-restore query. This means an expired access token but valid refresh token flips useMe() to error and any naive session gate will misroute the user. Fix: route meRequest() through apiGet so refresh resilience applies. Caught during review of mobile welcome + session gate plan (2026-05-24).

MLabs mobile auth uses TWO bearer-acceptable tokens with DIFFERENT validators: (1) Better Auth session token (mobile stores as 'refresh', 7d TTL) is accepted by Better Auth's built-in /api/auth/* routes via the bearer plugin (DB session lookup); (2) JWT access token (1h TTL) is accepted by our custom /api/v1/* routes via lib/auth/server.ts getSessionFromHeaders (stateless verify). They are NOT interchangeable. meRequest() hits /api/auth/get-session and MUST send the session token, not the JWT. Don't 'refactor' meRequest to use apiGet — apiGet attaches the JWT, which Better Auth rejects. Caught during code phase for mobile welcome+gate plan (2026-05-24).

Maestro test runner cannot write to SecureStore — there's no built-in primitive and the codebase has no dev-only seed-token hook. Tests that need a specific pre-existing session state must either (a) chain a real signup/login flow first to populate SecureStore organically (see flow 08-cold-launch-session-restore.yaml pattern), or (b) test the semantically-equivalent no-tokens scenario. Stale/expired-token cases specifically are not directly testable without adding a dev hook. Caught while writing mobile flow 12-deep-link-expired-session.yaml (2026-05-24).

When porting BetFrnd's e2e fixture back to the MLabs template, the user table schema differences matter: BetFrnd has username + isOver18 (additionalFields); MLabs has only the default BetterAuth columns (name, email, emailVerified, image, role). Cleanup strategy must adapt: BetFrnd uses 'DELETE WHERE username LIKE e2e-test-%'; the template uses 'DELETE WHERE id = E2E_TEST_USER.id' with a hardcoded UUID. Same crash-recovery property (next run wipes the stale row before re-inserting), no schema dependency on additionalFields. Pattern generalises: any fixture that includes a stable test user should use id-based cleanup, not column-based, so it survives schema drift across forks.

When porting Next.js API routes from a working fork into a template, always add 'export const runtime = "nodejs"' explicitly if the route uses any Node-only API (Stripe SDK's HMAC, db Pool sockets, fs, crypto.createHmac, etc.). Next 16 auto-selects runtime based on a heuristic that can wrongly land on edge for routes that LOOK lightweight. Edge-runtime 500s on first request are silent until prod traffic hits them. Cheap insurance, explicit at the file top. Caught during 2026-05-24 review of the Stripe webhook route port.

Playwright multi-project configs need explicit testIgnore on the default project when sibling projects use testMatch for opt-in specs. e.g. shipping {chromium: {}, authed: {testMatch: '**/*.authed.spec.ts'}} without testIgnore on chromium means chromium ALSO tries to run *.authed.spec.ts (without storageState) — they fail on the redirect-to-/login. Always pair: project A {testMatch: 'X'} + project B {testIgnore: ['X']}. Surfaced during 2026-05-24 review of the e2e fixture plan.
